scope.target: Open a dedicated workflow to separate synchronous Use Case objects from message or command Handlers
scope.dependency_radius: Controller invocation, Use Case contracts, Command/Query DTOs, synchronous handlers, MQ/event handlers, prompts, examples, tests, and analyzers
constraints: Do not execute inside the Repository Pattern standards implementation
Controller should depend on an application Use Case interface for synchronous API behavior
Do not assume publishing a Command is the default synchronous API invocation mechanism
Keep message/event Handler responsibilities distinct from Use Case responsibilities
non_goals: No Use Case or Handler standards rewrite in the Repository Pattern workflow

inputs.architecture_target: Controller -> IUseCase -> Use Case implementation as the synchronous API default, with Handler as a distinct role
review_findings: The canonical relationship document currently treats Handler as the default Use Case implementation
Current guidance contains competing invocation models
user_constraints: Use Case and Handler are separate independent objects
API Controller injects a Use Case interface
Normal synchronous API behavior does not publish a Command
Persist this follow-up so it is not forgotten

steps: Open a dedicated workflow after Repository Pattern alignment closes
Inventory all Use Case, Handler, Controller, Command, Query, and Wolverine role definitions
Resolve synchronous invocation and message-handler boundaries
Update canonical standards before prompts, examples, tests, and analyzers
Run cross-reference and validator consistency checks
validation: Dedicated workflow artifact exists before implementation
Controller, Use Case, Handler, and message-delivery roles have one canonical definition
Prompts, examples, tests, and analyzers match the approved invocation model
deferred_items: All implementation is deferred until the Repository Pattern workflow closes

results.summary: Recorded as an explicit follow-up because current standards conflict with the user's intended Use Case and Handler separation.
